name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ (١١٤)
```

```english
Certainly We favoured Moses and Aaron, (114)
```

```arabic
وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُمَا وَقَوْمَهُمَا مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (١١٥)
```

```english
and delivered them and their people from their great distress, (115)
```

```arabic
وَنَصَرْنَـٰهُمْ فَكَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ (١١٦)
```

```english
and We helped them so that they became the victors. (116)
```

```arabic
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ٱلْمُسْتَبِينَ (١١٧)
```

```english
We gave them the illuminating scripture (117)
```

```arabic
وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمَا ٱلصِّرَٰطَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ (١١٨)
```

```english
and guided them to the straight path, (118)
```

```arabic
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِمَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١١٩)
```

```english
and left for them a good name in posterity. (119)
```

```arabic
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ (١٢٠)
```

```english
‘Peace be to Moses and Aaron!’ (120)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٢١)
```

```english
Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous. (121)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُمَا مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٢٢)
```

```english
They are indeed among Our faithful servants. (122)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ إِلْيَاسَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٢٣)
```

```english
Indeed Ilyas was one of the apostles. (123)
```

```arabic
إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٢٤)
```

```english
When he said to his people, ‘Will you not be Godwary? (124)
```

```arabic
أَتَدْعُونَ بَعْلًا وَتَذَرُونَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْخَـٰلِقِينَ (١٢٥)
```

```english
Do you invoke Baal and abandon the best of creators, (125)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهَ رَبَّكُمْ وَرَبَّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٢٦)
```

```english
Allah, your Lord and Lord of your forefathers?,’ (126)
```

```arabic
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ (١٢٧)
```

```english
they impugned him. So they will indeed be mustered [in hell] (127)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (١٢٨)
```

```english
—[all] except Allah’s exclusive servants. (128)
```

```arabic
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٢٩)
```

```english
We left for him a good name in posterity. (129)
```

```arabic
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰٓ إِلْ يَاسِينَ (١٣٠)
```

```english
‘Peace be to Ilyas!’ (130)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٣١)
```

```english
Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous. (131)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٢)
```

```english
He is indeed one of Our faithful servants. (132)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a summary of the story of Moses (موسى) and Aaron (هارون), followed by an allusion to the story of Elijah (إلياس), peace be upon him (عليه السلام). It details the blessings Allah bestowed upon them and the punishment of those who denied them, noting that the aspect of mercy therein outweighs the aspect of punishment, and the glad tidings exceed the warnings.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلى مُوسى وَهارُونَ** "Certainly We certainly favored Moses and Aaron" (37:114). Favor (المن) refers to the bestowing of blessings (الإنعام). It is possible that what is intended here is the collective blessings He is about to enumerate—which He bestowed upon them and their people—such as deliverance, victory, the granting of the Book, guidance, and others. In this case, His saying: **وَنَجَّيْناهُما** "And We delivered them..." would be an explanatory apposition (عطف التفسير).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَجَّيْناهُما وَقَوْمَهُما مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ** "And We delivered them and their people from the great agony" (37:115). This refers to the intense distress (الغم الشديد) resulting from Pharaoh’s oppression of them, as he inflicted upon them a grievous punishment, slaughtering their sons and sparing their women.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَصَرْناهُمْ فَكانُوا هُمُ الْغالِبِينَ** "And We helped them, so they were the victors" (37:116). This assistance led to their departure from Egypt, their crossing of the sea, and the destruction of Pharaoh and his hosts.

By this, the misconception is dispelled that the apparent context should have mentioned "victory" before "deliverance," since the latter depends on the former. This is because victory (النصر) occurs when the one being helped possesses some degree of power, yet it is insufficient to repel evil until it is completed by divine aid. When the Children of Israel departed Egypt, they possessed some measure of strength; thus, it was appropriate to apply the term "victory" to the assistance given to them in that regard. This contrasts with the fundamental act of their liberation from Pharaoh’s hand, for they were captives and enslaved, possessing no power of their own. In this respect, it was only appropriate to mention "deliverance" (التنجية) rather than "victory."

His saying, the Exalted: **وَآتَيْناهُمَا الْكِتابَ الْمُسْتَبِينَ** "And We gave them the manifest Book" (37:117). That is, it clarifies hidden unknowns and elucidates them; these are the matters people require for their worldly life and their hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَهَدَيْناهُمَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ** "And We guided them to the straight path" (37:118). What is intended here is guidance (الهداية) in the full sense of the word. For this reason, He specified the two of them with it and did not include their people alongside them. We have previously discussed the meaning of guidance to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — in Surah al-Fatihah.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَتَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِما فِي الْآخِرِينَ** "And We left for them [a good name] among posterity" (37:119) until His saying: **الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "...the faithful" (37:122). The commentary on these verses has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنَّ إِلْياسَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** "Indeed Elijah was one of the apostles" (37:123). It is said that he (عليه السلام) was from the lineage of Aaron and was sent to Baalbek; however, the author did not mention anything in his discourse to substantiate this.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ قالَ لِقَوْمِهِ أَلا تَتَّقُونَ أَتَدْعُونَ بَعْلاً وَتَذَرُونَ أَحْسَنَ الْخالِقِينَ** "When he said to his people, ‘Will you not be God-wary? Do you invoke Baal and abandon the Best of creators—" (37:124-125) until His saying: **الْأَوَّلِينَ** "...the forefathers?" (37:126). This is a portion of his (عليه السلام) mission, in which he calls his people to monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and rebukes them for worshipping Baal—an idol they possessed—and abandoning the worship of Allah, the Glorified.

His speech (عليه السلام), despite the rebuke and blame it contains, comprises a complete proof for His Oneness, the Exalted. For his saying: **وَتَذَرُونَ أَحْسَنَ الْخالِقِينَ اللهَ رَبَّكُمْ وَرَبَّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** "and abandon the Best of creators, Allah, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers?" (37:125-126) first rebukes them for abandoning the worship of the Best of creators. Creation (الخلق) and origination (الإيجاد), just as they relate to the essences of things, also relate to the system governing them, which is termed "governance" (التدبير). Thus, just as creation belongs to Him, the Exalted, governance also belongs to Him; He is the Governor (المدبر) just as He is the Creator (الخالق). He alluded to this by His saying: **اللهَ رَبَّكُمْ** "Allah, your Lord" after describing Him, the Exalted, as the Best of creators.

Then he pointed out that His Lordship (الربوبية), the Exalted, is not exclusive to one people over another—unlike idols, of which each people adopts some to the exclusion of others, such that an idol might be a lord for one people but not for others. Rather, He, the Exalted, is the Lord for them and for their forefathers; He is not exclusive to some over others due to the universality of His creation and governance. To this he alluded by his saying: **اللهَ رَبَّكُمْ وَرَبَّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** "Allah, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers."

His saying, the Exalted: **فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ** "But they impugned him. So they will indeed be arraigned [for punishment]" (37:127). That is, they will be resurrected to be brought (ليحضروا) to the punishment. It has been previously mentioned that the term "arraignment" (الإحضار), when used absolutely, conveys the sense of evil.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** "except the chosen servants of Allah" (37:128). This is evidence that there was a group among his people who were of this description.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَتَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ** "And We left for him [a good name] among posterity" (37:129) until His saying: **الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "...the faithful" (37:132). The discussion regarding similar verses has preceded.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَتَدْعُونَ بَعْلاً** "Do you invoke Baal," he said: "They had an idol they called Baal."

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of transmission to Qadih, from Imam al-Sadiq, Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his father, from his forefathers, from Imam Ali (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **سلام على آل يس** "Peace be upon the progeny of Yasin." He said: **يس محمد صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله ونحن آل يس** "Yasin is Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and we are the progeny of Yasin (آل يس)."

I say: A similar report is narrated in *al-Uyun* from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام). This interpretation is based on the recitation of **آل يس** (the progeny of Yasin), as recited by Nafi', Ibn 'Amir, Ya'qub, and Zayd.

## (A Discourse on the Story of Elijah (عليه السلام))

**1. His Story in the Quran:** His name (عليه السلام) is not mentioned in the Noble Quran except in this place and in Surah al-An'am, during the mention of the guidance of the Prophets, where He says: **وَزَكَرِيَّا وَيَحْيى وَعِيسى وَإِلْياسَ كُلٌّ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** "And Zechariah, John, Jesus and Elijah; each was one of the righteous" (6:85).

The Exalted did not mention anything of his story in this Surah except that he used to call a people who worshipped Baal to the worship of Allah, the Glorified. A group of them believed in him with sincere faith, while others—who were the majority of the people—denied him, and they shall indeed be arraigned.

Allah, the Glorified, praised him in Surah al-An'am with the same praise He gave to the Prophets in general, and He praised him in this Surah as being among His faithful, virtuous servants, and He greeted him with peace, based on the well-known recitation: **سَلامٌ عَلى إِلْ ياسِينَ** "Peace be upon Ilyasin" (37:130).

**2. The Narrations Concerning Him:** Various conflicting reports have been narrated regarding him (عليه السلام), as is the case with most reports concerning the stories of the Prophets that recount wonders. For instance, it is narrated from Ibn Mas'ud that Elijah is Idris; and from Ibn Abbas, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), that al-Khidr is Elijah. It is narrated from Wahb, Ka'b al-Ahbar, and others that Elijah is alive and will not die until the first blast of the Trumpet. From Wahb, it is said that Elijah asked Allah to relieve him of his people, so Allah sent him a beast in the form of a horse the color of fire; he leaped upon it and it departed with him, and Allah clothed him in feathers and light, and removed from him the pleasure of food and drink, so he became among the angels. From Ka'b al-Ahbar, it is said that Elijah is the master of the mountains and the wilderness, and that he is the one whom Allah named Dhu al-Nun. From al-Hasan, it is said that Elijah is entrusted with the deserts and al-Khidr is entrusted with the mountains. From Anas, it is said that Elijah met the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) during one of his journeys; they sat and conversed, then a table descended upon them from heaven, they ate and fed me, then he bid him and me farewell, and then I saw him passing over the clouds toward the sky—among other such reports. In some Shi'a reports, it is mentioned that he (عليه السلام) is alive and immortal, but these are weak, and the apparent meaning of the verses of the story does not support this.

In *al-Bihar*, regarding the story of Elijah (عليه السلام), from *Qisas al-Anbiya*, with a chain of transmission to al-Saduq, from Wahb ibn Munabbih—and al-Tha'labi narrated it in *al-Ara'is* from Ibn Ishaq, and the chroniclers of reports have it in more detail—the tradition is very long, and its summary is: After the kingdom of the Children of Israel split and was divided among them, a tribe of them traveled to Baalbek. They had a king from among them who worshipped an idol named Baal and compelled the people to worship it.

He had a profligate wife who had married seven kings before him and had given birth to ninety children, besides grandchildren. The king would appoint her as his deputy when he was absent, and she would judge among the people. He had a wise, believing scribe who had saved three hundred believers from her hand when she intended to kill them. Neighboring the king's palace was a believing man who owned a garden, and the king used to respect his neighborliness and honor him.

During one of the king's absences, the woman killed the believing neighbor and usurped his garden. When the king returned and learned of this, he rebuked her, but she offered excuses and satisfied him. Consequently, Allah, the Exalted, took an oath upon Himself to take vengeance upon them if they did not repent. He sent Elijah (عليه السلام) to them, calling them to the worship of Allah and informing them of Allah's oath. Their anger toward him intensified, and they intended to torture and kill him. He fled from them to the most rugged mountain there and remained in it for seven years, living on the plants of the earth and the fruits of the trees.

Then Allah caused a son of the king, whom he loved dearly, to fall ill. He sought intercession from Baal, but it did not avail him. It was said to him: "He is angry with you because you have not killed Elijah." So he sent a group of his people to deceive him and capture him. Allah sent a fire upon them and burned them. Then he sent another group of powerful men along with his believing scribe. Elijah went with him to protect him from the king's wrath, but Allah, the Glorified, caused the king's son to die, and his grief distracted him from Elijah, who returned safely.

When time passed, Elijah descended from the mountain and hid in the house of the mother of Jonah (يونس) ibn Matta, while Jonah was a nursing infant. After six months, he returned to the mountain. It happened that Jonah died after that, but Allah brought him back to life through the prayer of Elijah after his mother went out seeking him, found him, and pleaded with him.

Then he asked Allah to take vengeance for him against the Children of Israel and to withhold the rain from them. His prayer was answered, and Allah afflicted them with famine, and they suffered for years. They eventually regretted, came to him, repented, and embraced Islam. He prayed to Allah, and He sent rain upon them, quenching their thirst and reviving their lands.

They complained to him about the destruction of their walls and the lack of seeds for grain. It was revealed to him to command them to sow salt, and He caused chickpeas to grow for them; and to sow sand, and He caused millet to grow for them from it.

Then, when Allah removed the distress from them, they broke the covenant and returned to a state more wicked than they were before. Elijah became weary of this and prayed to Allah to relieve him of them. Allah sent him a horse of fire; Elijah leaped upon it, and Allah raised him to heaven, clothing him in feathers and light, so he was with the angels.

Then Allah set an enemy upon the king and his wife, who sought them out, prevailed over them, and killed them. He threw their carcasses into the garden of that believing man whom they had killed and whose garden they had usurped.

By reflecting upon what this narration recounts, you will have no doubt regarding its weakness.
